title: A missing causal principle: Coordination
authors: Daniel Centeno, Antoine Coquet, Maria Ciudad Alañón, Lucas Tendick, Marc-Olivier Renou, Elie Wolfe
abstract: We introduce the coordination principle, which states that perfect coordination, in the form of agreement on a uniformly random output, among N parties is possible only if they share a common cause. This principle is purely causal and can be viewed as a multipartite generalization of Reichenbach's common cause principle. We prove that quantum information theory satisfy the coordination principle in any network, and derive noise-tolerant Bell-like inequalities that certify the presence of a common cause. We further show that the principle is not a consequence of no-signaling and independence alone by constructing a concrete operational probabilistic theory that obeys both principles while still allowing perfect coordination without a common cause. This possibility arises only in fully general causal scenarios with intermediate transformations between preparations and measurements. We also formulate a genuinely quantum coordination task, showing that the preparation of a multipartite GHZ state requires a quantum common cause, which can be certified by Bell-like inequalities which are experimentally testable. Finally, we discuss the open problem of finding a quantitative, noise-tolerant version of the coordination principle that constrains approximate coordination in any reasonable causal theory. This work is the extended version of the more compact letter and provides all the technical details of the proofs.
summary: This paper introduces the Coordination Principle, stating that perfect agreement among multiple parties implies a shared common cause. It proves that quantum mechanics obeys this principle even in complex causal networks and provides new inequalities to experimentally certify common causes in multipartite GHZ states.
main_problem: The paper investigates whether the 'Coordination Principle'—the idea that perfect agreement on a random output among N parties requires a shared common cause—is a fundamental causal principle or merely a consequence of No-Signaling and Independence.
main_result: The authors prove that quantum theory satisfies the Coordination Principle in any causal network, even with intermediate transformations, and derive noise-tolerant Bell-like inequalities to certify the presence of a common cause.
method: The study employs the 'Quantum Inflation' technique to expand causal structures, uses the NPA hierarchy for semidefinite programming, and constructs a counterexample using an Operational Probabilistic Theory (OPT) to show the principle is independent of No-Signaling.
model_or_system: The framework uses Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs) to represent causal structures, specifically studying multipartite GHZ states and complex scenarios like the two-layer tetrahedron model.
key_observables: Correlations between adjacent parties (e.g., <Ai Ai+1>) and end-to-end correlations (e.g., <A1 AN>), as well as marginal probabilities.
important_parameters: The number of parties (N), the white noise visibility parameter (v), and the fidelity (f) of the GHZ state.
main_assumptions: The proofs assume the use of pure states, unitary transformations, and projective measurements; it also assumes the possibility of device replication in certain contexts.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate various causal DAG structures, the quantum cut inflation construction, diagrammatic representations of tests and circuits in the counterexample OPT, and plots showing the violation of inequalities as a function of noise visibility.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the Coordination Principle, proves quantum theory satisfies it using inflation techniques, provides a counterexample OPT where the principle fails, derives noise-robust Bell-like inequalities, and applies these to GHZ state certification.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for researchers in quantum networks and quantum optics as it provides new experimental tools (Bell-like inequalities) to certify the existence of shared quantum resources and common causes in multipartite systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Various causal structures represented as directed acyclic graphs (DAGs). Squares denote observed nodes and circles denote latent nodes. Latent nodes represent either sources of information (when parentless) or transformations of information (when they have parents), while observed nodes correspond to measurements. The label notation of the nodes is chosen to make causal relationships explicit. Concretely, we label latent nodes by the complement of the set of observed nodes in their future, denoted ¬(·), whereas the observed nodes are simply denoted by A1,...,AN. Reichenbach’s common cause principle rules out perfect coordination (indeed, any non-factorizing distribution) in (a),...

FIG. 2. Graphical representation of the quantum cut inflation of the two-layer tetrahedron. The superindex in parenthesis indicate the copy index of the sources in the past of the nodes. The black dots denote whenever the same Hilbert space of a given source state is pertinent in defining more than one transformation. Accordingly, pairs of transformations related by a black dot (i.e., ¬(A1A3)(1,1) and ¬(A1A3)(2,1) or ¬(A2A4)(1,1) and ¬(A2A4)(1,2)) cannot be physically implemented simultaneously. That implies incompatibility of their downstream measurements (A2 and A4 or A1 and A3, respectively). Nevertheless, while the whole quantum inflation is not physically valid, certain parts of it...
FIG. 4. Diagrammatic representation of parallel composition.
FIG. 7. Different preparations available within the OPT T . Different types of systems are labeled by a number next to the wire.
FIG. 5. Diagrammatic representation of sequential composition.
